attestation.statement: Clinician (clinical sign-off authority) confirmed having clinically reviewed all 50 DST (Dermatology & Soft Tissue) answer keys in this batch and attests them clinically correct, and authorised ingest. Bidirectional tier calibration reviewed and accepted: benign / self-limiting dermatoses held low-tier (T1/T2, self-care + routine referral), while skin malignancy (melanoma/SCC/BCC), spreading soft-tissue infection, limb-threat (critical limb ischaemia, diabetic foot ulcer) and airway/anaphylaxis risk were escalated to true acuity (T3/T4) with mandatory escalation edges; no autonomous diagnosis, no paediatric dosing.
attestation.recorded_by: claude-opus-4-8 (agent, on clinician's explicit written confirmation in-session)
attestation.scope: all DST casebundles in this batch (n=50)

codes_manifest.display: Herpesviral gingivostomatitis and pharyngotonsillitis (candidate — primary diagnosis)
Hand, foot and mouth disease (candidate — differential)

transform_flags: Age inferred as a young adult (~24) — the source note does not state an exact age but describes a 'young adult' with 68 kg body weight and an independent baseline; adult band, so no paediatric dosing issue. Please confirm on clinician review.

Sex inferred as female with NO firm basis in the source note (no gendered detail is given) — recorded only because the schema requires sex_at_birth; flag for clinician confirmation/correction. Does not affect the diagnosis, tier or scoring.
Geographic context and occupation not stated in the source — omitted rather than guessed.

Telehealth reprojection applied: the source is an in-person after-hours clinician who administered 10 mL viscous lidocaine 2%, personally supervised a 200 mL oral fluid trial (patient tolerated it), measured vitals and examined/palpated the mouth and submandibular nodes. Patient/video-observable findings (broken lip blisters and raw crusted sores, red swollen gums and mouth ulcers visible on video; ~18 hours of no fluids and drooling; reduced urine, dry mouth, weakness/lightheadedness; caregiver impression of pallor/exhaustion/fever) routed to 01.objective_data_offered (verified:false, source-channel tagged). Clinician-measured vitals (BP 108/68, HR 105, Temp 38.1, RR 18, SpO2 98%, Wt 68 kg), capillary refill ~3 s, the detailed oral exam and submandibular lymphadenopathy, and the un-examinable airway/deep-space assessment are sealed in 10/11 with telehealth_limits. The in-person analgesia + supervised fluid trial + autonomous prescribing reprojected to recognise-indication + arrange urgent same-day in-person care; IV rehydration reprojected to an ED escalation edge.

De-anchoring applied: the source filename/header name the diagnosis (herpes simplex / cold sores) and it is a first-ever PRIMARY infection the patient would not self-label. The green side (01/02) carries only the lay presentation (a first-ever, severe, painful blistering/ulcerating mouth-and-lip eruption after days of feeling unwell, unable to swallow fluids, dehydrating) with no diagnosis label. The full primary_diagnosis.name does not appear in any injectable field.

Tier set to T3 (urgent same-day in-person assessment), NOT routine self-care and NOT a reflexive 000: this is a SEVERE PRIMARY oral HSV infection with a functional inability to maintain hydration and early dehydration (source Risk Level noted moderate for dehydration; the source's own concern was impending hospitalisation for IV rehydration). Telehealth cannot deliver the topical analgesia, supervised fluid trial, prescribing or IV fluids that made the in-person home plan work, so urgent same-day in-person care is the proportionate disposition, with mandatory escalation to ED (T4) for failure to rehydrate and to 000 (T5) for airway compromise or collapse. The patient is haemodynamically stable and alert, so a reflexive 000 (T5) is over-triage while a direct-to-ED (T4) choice is an acceptable more-cautious alternative. Under-triage to routine cold-sore self-care (T0/T1) is the primary scored failure; minimum_viable_tier_for_pass set to T3.

Candidate SNOMED/ICD-10-AM codes recorded for the primary diagnosis and differentials with verification_status unverified_pending_terminology_receipt; none claimed verified. SNOMED 1348009 (herpetic gingivostomatitis) and the differential SNOMED codes are best-effort candidates for later terminology-receipt confirmation; ICD-10-AM B00.2 (Herpesviral gingivostomatitis and pharyngotonsillitis) is the candidate primary code.

Drug AMT/PBS codes for valaciclovir, viscous lidocaine 2%, paracetamol and ibuprofen were NOT assigned (candidate pending terminology receipt) rather than fabricated; amt_snomed_code omitted on the medication objects.

Doses: valaciclovir 1000 mg twice daily for 7 days and viscous lidocaine 2% (10 mL, clinician-administered) taken verbatim from the source note; none synthesised. The source specified 'regular paracetamol' and 'regular ibuprofen' as liquids with NO milligram dose — these were left as standard adult OTC dosing per packet directions rather than synthesising a specific dose. Ibuprofen flagged as a relative contraindication in dehydration (AKI risk) with paracetamol preferred, a clinical caution added on transform (the source prescribed both).
Provisional case_id SPEC-DERM-04-00223 — maintainer to confirm/reassign the SEQ within the SPEC-DERM-04 bucket via ingest --reseq.

Difficulty tier set to atypical_presentation_high_risk (04): the diagnosis itself is a classic primary-HSV constellation, but a folder-labelled 'cold sore' presenting as a high-risk severe primary infection with dehydration and must-not-miss SJS/airway mimics is a deliberate over/under-triage-calibration case; the '04' high-risk framing signals it must not be under-triaged.

ingest.hashing: SHA-256 over canonical (JSON 2-space + trailing newline) bytes of each split node
